coredata.dc:title: Microwave-induced combustion synthesis of Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 (M=Al, Cr, Mn) powder and their characterization
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Yen-Pei
Tsao, Shen
Hu, Chen-Ti
Yao, Yeong-Der
coredata.dc:description: Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 (M=Al, Cr, Mn) powders with small and uniformly sized particles were successfully synthesized by microwave-induced combustion, using lithium nitrate, ferric nitrate, aluminum nitrate, chromium nitrate, manganese nitrate, and carbohydrazide as the starting materials. The process takes only a few minutes to obtain as-received Al, Cr, and Mn-substituted lithium ferrite powders. The resultant powders annealed at 650°C for 2h and were investigated by thermogravimeter/differential thermal analyzer (TG/DTA), X-ray diffractometer (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), and thermomagnetic analysis (TMA). The results revealed that saturation magnetization and Curie temperature of Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 were strongly affected by different metal ions substitution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium ferrite
Microwave-induced combustion synthesis
Saturation magnetization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DTA/TG curves for as-received Li0.5Fe2.5O4 powders.
X-ray powder pattern of as-received and annealed Li0.5Fe2.5O4 powders at different temperatures.
X-ray powder pattern of Li0.5Fe2.0M0.5O4 (M=Al, Cr, Mn) powders prepared by microwave-induced combustion followed by annealing at 650°C for 2h.
The lattice parameter of Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 powder prepared by microwave-induced combustion followed by annealing at 650°C for 2h.
The saturation magnetization as a function of M (Al, Cr, Mn) content for Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4.
Curie temperature for Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 powders as a function of M (Al, Cr, Mn) content.
TEM micrograph of (a) Li0.5Fe2.5O4, (b) Li0.5Fe2.0Al0.5O4, (c) Li0.5Fe2.0Cr0.5O4, and (d) Li0.5Fe2.0Mn0.5O4 powders prepared by microwave-induced combustion followed by annealing at 650°C for 2h.
Magnetic properties of Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 (M=Al, Cr, Mn) prepared by microwave-induced combustion and annealed at 650°C for 2h, an applied magnetic field of 12kOe measured at room temperature

article.ce:title: Microwave-induced combustion synthesis of Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 (M=Al, Cr, Mn) powder and their characterization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Wu-Feng Institute of Technology, Ming-Hsiung, Chiayi 621, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Tsing-Hua University, Hsinchu 300, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physics, Academia Sinica, Taipei 115, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 (M=Al, Cr, Mn) powders with small and uniformly sized particles were successfully synthesized by microwave-induced combustion, using lithium nitrate, ferric nitrate, aluminum nitrate, chromium nitrate, manganese nitrate, and carbohydrazide as the starting materials. The process takes only a few minutes to obtain as-received Al, Cr, and Mn-substituted lithium ferrite powders. The resultant powders annealed at 650°C for 2h and were investigated by thermogravimeter/differential thermal analyzer (TG/DTA), X-ray diffractometer (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), and thermomagnetic analysis (TMA). The results revealed that saturation magnetization and Curie temperature of Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 were strongly affected by different metal ions substitution.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium ferrites have attracted the attention of scientists for a long time and been developed as a replacement for yttrium iron garnet (YIG) due to their low cost [1]. They are important components for the microwave devices and memory core owing to their high-Curie temperature, high-saturation magnetization and hysteresis loop properties offer performance advantage over other spinel structure [2]. The conventional way of producing these materials is by the solid-state reaction of oxide/carbonate and calcined at high temperature. The solid-state reaction method has some inherent disadvantages: such as (1) chemical inhomogeneity, (2) coarser particle size, and (3) introduction of impurities during ball milling [3]. Furthermore, the high-temperature induced the lower magnetization due to the precipitation of α-Fe2O3 or the formation of Fe3O4 [4]. Several papers have been reported with regard to their electrical, magnetic, and structure properties of lithium ferrite by different method [5–7]. They include coprecipitation, sol–gel synthesis, alkoxide hydrolysis, hydrothermal reaction, and citrate route. In this study, we attempted a new method, called microwave-induced combustion synthesis process to produce Al, Cr and Mn-substituted lithium ferrite powders.

Microwave processing of materials is fundamentally different from the conventional processing in its heat mechanism. In a microwave oven, heat is generated within the sample itself by the interaction of microwaves with the material. In a conventional heating, the heat is generated by heating elements and then it is transferred to the sample surfaces [8].

The combustion synthesis process is to dissolve metal nitrate and carbohydrazide in water [9], and then to heat the solution in a microwave oven. The carbohydrazide and metal nitrate decomposing and give flammable gases such as NH3, HNCO, and NO, respectively. After the solution reaches the point of spontaneous combustion, it begins burning and becomes a solid, which burns at high temperature. The combustion is not finished until all the flammable substances are all burned out and it turns out to be a loose substances which show voids, pores, and high friable formed by the escaping gases during the combustion reaction. The whole process takes only a few minutes to yield powders of lithium ferrite. The effect of different metal-substituted on magnetic properties and Curie temperature for Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 (M=Al, Cr, Mn) specimens were studied in this paper.

The synthesis process involved the combustion of redox mixtures, in which metal nitrate acted as an oxidizing reactant and carbohydrazide as a reducing one. The initial composition of the solution containing lithium nitrate, ferric nitrate, aluminum nitrate, chromium nitrate, manganese nitrate, and carbohydrazide was based on the total oxidizing and reducing valences of the oxidizer and the fuel using the concepts of the propellant chemistry [10]. The detail process is described as follows. According to the stoichiometric composition of Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 (M=Al, Cr, Mn) (x =0.0, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0), specified amount of lithium nitrate [LiNO3], ferric nitrate [Fe(NO3)3·9H2O], aluminum nitrate [Al(NO3)3·9H2O], chromium nitrate [Cr(NO3)3·9H2O], manganese nitrate [Mn(NO3)2·6H2O], and carbohydrazide [CO(N2H3)2] were dissolved in a minimum quantity of water, which were set in a crucible. The crucible containing the solution was introduced into a microwave oven (CEM, MDS 81D, 650W). Initially, the solution boils and undergoes dehydration followed by decomposition with the evolution of large amount of gases (NO, NH3, and HNCO). After the solution reaches the point of spontaneous combustion, it begins burning and releases lots of heat, vaporizes all the solution instantly and becomes a solid burning at high temperature. The entire combustion process produces Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 (M=Al, Cr, Mn) powders in a microwave oven takes only 15min. A stoichiometric combustion reaction of metal nitrate with carbohydrazide to form lithium ferrites as follow:
0.5 LiN
O 3
+ 2.5 Fe
( N
O 3
)
3
+ 5.0 CO
(
N 2
H 3
)

i

e
2.5
O 4
+ 5.0 C
O 2
+ 14.0
N 2
+ 15.0
H 2
O

A computerized X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation (Rigaku D/Max-II) was used to identify the crystalline phase. The particle shape, size, and microstructure were imaged by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-200CX) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. Differential thermal analysis and thermogravimetry (DTA/TG, Rigaku Thermalplus TG 8120) were used to study the exo-endo temperature of as-received Li0.5Fe2.5O4 powder. A heating rate of 10°C/min was used in both the DTA and TG measurements up to 1000°C in air. Thermal analysis is done under flowing air at 100ml/min. A vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) was used to measure the saturation magnetization (Ms) and intrinsic coercive force (Hc) of the annealed Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 (M=Al, Cr, Mn) powders at room temperature. The magnetic phases and the corresponding Curie temperature (T c) were measured by a thermal gravimetric analyzer with an externally applied magnetic field (conventionally referred to as “TMA”).

Phase transformation of as-received lithium ferrite powders was studied using DTA/TG measurements. Fig. 1 shows the DTA/TG curves for as-received Li0.5Fe2.5O4 powders. The TG data of as-received Li0.5Fe2.5O4 powder prepared by microwave-induced combustion yield three steps from 25 to 900°C. First step is from room temperature to 400°C, which is ascribed to the decomposition of absorbed water. Second step is from 400 to 700°C, which is associated with the redox reactions of the organic matter involved in carbohydrazide. The weight loss below 700°C is due to loss of absorbed water and the decomposition of organic derivatives. Third step is above 700°C, which the weight of as-received Li0.5Fe2.5O4 powder appears to be constant. Moreover, the DTA curve does not show evident endothermic or exothermic peaks during all temperatures.

Fig. 2 illustrates the X-ray diffraction patterns of the as-received Li0.5Fe2.5O4 powder and as-received Li0.5Fe2.5O4 samples annealed at different temperatures. It is evident that the annealed as-received Li0.5Fe2.5O4 powders contain the lithium ferrite phase. Lower annealing temperature leads to a poor crystallinity, whereas much better crystallized powders are given above 550°C. As the annealing temperature from 350 to 550°C, the products are composed of spinel type Li0.5Fe2.5O4 with a little of α-Fe2O3. As the annealing temperature at 650 and 750°C, Li0.5Fe2.5O4 formed all the peaks in the pattern, matching well with JCPDS card. No secondary phases are detected in the XRD patterns of samples. Further increasing the annealing temperature to 850°C results in an augmentation of α-Fe2O3.

Fig. 3 reveals the X-ray powder diffraction pattern of Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 (M=Al, Cr, Mn) powder prepared by microwave-induced combustion followed by annealed at 650°C for 2h. All specimens reveal single-phase ferrite with spinel structure. No phase change could be identified in XRD patterns. The introduction of different metal ions into Li0.5Fe2.5O4 can cause a small shift in the lithium ferrite peaks. This shift is indicative of the change in lattice parameter. Fig. 4 shows the dependence of lattice constant versus different metal ions dopant concentration. As the Mn content increase, the lattice constant increases with the content of Mn for Li0.5Fe2.5−x Mn x O4 (x =0.0–1.0). It indicates different radii of Fe3+ (0.64Å) and Mn2+ (0.80Å) in an oxide solid solution with a spinel-type structure. When doped Mn2+ ions with larger sized in lithium ferrite, cause the spinel lithium ferrite swell. Doping Mn2+ ions in a spinel-type structure will induce uniform strain in the lattice as the material is elastically deformed. This effect causes the lattice plane spacing to change and the diffraction peaks to shift to a lower 2θ position.

Moreover, As the Al and Cr content increase, the lattice constant decreases with the content of Al and Cr for Li0.5Fe2.5−x Al x O4 and Li0.5Fe2.5−x Cr x O4, respectively. It indicates that the ionic radii of Al3+ (0.51Å) and Cr3+ (0.63Å) are smaller than that of Fe3+ (0.64Å). When doped with smaller sized Al3+ or Cr3+ ions, the spinel lithium ferrite will shrink. Doping Al3+ or Cr3+ ions in a spinel-type structure will induce uniform strain in the lattice as the material is elastically deformed. This effect causes the lattice plane spacing to change and the diffraction peaks to shift to a higher 2θ position.

The magnetization measurements for the Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 (M=Al, Cr, Mn) specimens were carried out using a vibrating-sample magnetometer at room temperature with an applied magnetic field of 12kOe to reach saturation values. Fig. 5 plots the saturation magnetization as a function of content for Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 (M=Al, Cr, Mn) specimens. It indicates that Li0.5Fe2.5O4 prepared by microwave-induced combustion and followed by annealed at 650°C for 2h exhibits highest an optimum magnetic property, saturation magnetization of 71emu/g, remanent magnetization of 21emu/g, and intrinsic coercive force of 175Oe. Such high-saturation magnetization for as-received Li0.5Fe2.5O4 annealing at 650°C may be attributed to the high-phase purity and well-defined crystallinity. The saturation magnetization of Li0.5Fe2.5−x Al x O4 specimens decreases monotonously with increasing Al content from x =0.0 to x =1.0, the saturation magnetization decreased from 71 to 5emu/g. The saturation magnetization of Li0.5Fe2.5−x Cr x O4 specimens decreases gradually with increasing Cr content from x =0.0 to x =1.0, the saturation magnetization decreased from 71 to 8emu/g. However, the saturation magnetization of Li0.5Fe2.5−x Mn x O4 specimens initially decreases steadily with increasing Mn content from x =0.0 to x =0.2, and then further increasing Mn content led to a little rise in saturation magnetization to 58emu/g at x =0.5. As increasing the Mn content above x =0.8 demonstrates a monotonously fall in saturation magnetization to 46 and 42emu/g at x =0.8 and x =1.0, respectively. Introduction of Mn2+ ions into lithium ferrite greatly affects the magnetic properties such as the saturation magnetization, coercive force, and remanent magnetization.

Fig. 6 illustrates the Curie temperature of Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 (M=Al, Cr, Mn) powders as a function of different metal ions content, revealing that the Curie temperature decreases gradually with increasing different metal ions content from x =0.0 to x =1.0, the Curie temperature decreases from 640 to 445, 353, and 457°C for Al, Cr, and Mn substitution, respectively. This is ascribed to the reason that as increasing with the concentration of non-magnetic ions weakens the inter-site exchange interaction, and the value of Curie temperature decreases. The main magnetic properties of Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 (M=Al, Cr, Mn) powders are listed in Table 1 .

Fig. 7(a)–(d) presents TEM micrograph of lithium ferrite containing with different metal ions substitution. As shown in Fig. 7(a), Li0.5Fe2.5O4 powders reveal a range of size from 50 to 80nm with well-defined crystallinity. These particles are found to be stacked on top of each other due to their magnetic attraction. As shown in Fig. 7(b), Li0.5Fe2.0Al0.5O4 powders reveal a range of size from 30 to 40nm. This behavior is agreement with the crystallite size measured by XRD broadened peak, which the particle size is significantly dependent on the Al content in Li0.5Fe2.5Al x O4 specimens. As shown in Fig. 7(c), Li0.5Fe2.0Cr0.5O4 powders reveal a range of size from 30 to 80nm. As shown in Fig. 7(d), Li0.5Fe2.0Mn0.5O4 powders reveal a relatively uniform particle size distribution from 70 to 80nm with polyhedral shape.

A series of Al, Cr, and Mn-substituted lithium ferrite powder were successful synthesized by microwave-induced combustion process by using metal nitrate, and carbohydrazide as the starting materials. The effect of Al, Cr, Mn on magnetic properties and Curie temperature for stoichiometric Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 samples were investigated in this paper. Experimental results revealed that the saturation magnetization decreases with the increasing of nonmagnetic substitutes (Al, Cr, Mn) in Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 specimens, and the Curie temperature coercive force, and remanent magnetization of Li0.5Fe2.5−x M x O4 were also strongly affected by different metals ions substitution.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to express our appreciation to Professor W.C. Chang (National Chung Cheng University) and Dr. H.W. Chang (National Chung Cheng University) for his assistance on the magnetic measurements. The authors also would like to thank the National Science Council of the Republic of China for financially supporting this research under Contract No. NSC 93-2622-E-274-005-CC3.
